course_description:
Husbandry, health and nutrition of farm animals including common diseases and how farm management impacts on disease.

This course provides students with the opportunity to learn about the overall role of veterinary technologists in large animal health and management. This includes the opportunities to develop technical skills in handling and husbandry procedures, clinical examination, collection of diagnostic samples, and therapeutic procedures in large animals. The main focus of the lectures is on bovine and equine health management, but will also include other species such as sheep, goats, pigs and camelids. The lecture material will be complemented by practicals including: handling and clinical examination (horses and ruminants), diagnostic sampling and medication procedures (horses and ruminants), parasitology, and species-specific herd health procedures (dairy cattle, beef cattleﾠand sheep). Professionalism is expected during all learning activities.ﾠ

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
Completion of BVetTech Core Courses and successful completion of Pre-Clinical Extra-mural Studies (PEMS).
companions: You'll need to complete the following courses at the same time:

Additional timetable information

Please note that unexpected changes to the Timetable may occur throughout the Semester. Therefore, it is crucial to regularly check the course Blackboard site. Any updates to the timetable will be communicated to students via Blackboard Announcements.
course_aims: The course aims to provide veterinary technology students with an opportunity to develop an understanding of the management of large animal health problems and to develop technical skills in the application of this knowledge.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of the common health and management problems encountered in horses, cattle, small ruminants, pigs and camelids and be able to describe the management or treatment options for these problems.

Demonstrate knowledge and competence in the skills and procedures involved in the diagnosis and management of large animal species health problems including safely performing physical examinations, diagnostic procedures, and medication techniques of those species.

Demonstrate knowledge of the execution of a post-mortem examination in large animal species, understand the zoonotic potential of some large animal diseases and be able to apply the principles of infection control and biosecurity to prevent zoonoses.
Demonstrate the ability to identify parasites of importance in large animals and be able to describe their control and successful management when identified.

Students will be able to work effectively in small groups to plan, develop, and deliver veterinary practice relevant tasks, demonstrating professional communication, shared responsibility, and collaborative problem-solving appropriate to veterinary technology roles.

assessment_details.title: Assessment Sequence: Practical and Tutorial Quizzes
assessment_details.due_date: Due at the end of applicable practical session.
assessment_details.task_description: The Quizzes will be held at the end of the practical and tutorial sessions. Sheep handling and clinical examination (practical) - 4 questions Equine handling (practical) - 4 questions Equine clinical examination (practical) - 4 questions Cattle clinical examination (practical) - 4 questions Cattle procedures (practical) - 4 questions Equine dentistry & radiology (tutorial) - 3 questions each tutorial Equine procedures and bandaging (practical) - 4 questions These quizzes are to be submitted by the end of the practical or tutorial to the corresponding instructor. Each question is worth 1 mark to a total of 30 marks across the listed activities and this will contribute 25% towards the final mark for VETS3018. The results will be posted on the course Blackboard Ultra site. NOTE: There is no quiz associated with the needle and syringe skills tutorial. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
These quizzes are to be submitted at the end of the practical or tutorial to the corresponding instructor.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Students must be available for their examinations on the scheduled date, time, and campus of their course enrolment. The exam format is determined by the Course Coordinator and may include written (paper-based, online, or digital), practical, oral, or other timed assessments (such as quizzes).
If you are unable to sit for your original examination, you may be eligible for a deferred examination. To apply, you must demonstrate that unavoidable circumstances prevented you from attending your exam. If this is not possible, you can apply for a one-off discretionary deferred exam. All requests for deferred examinations MUST include supporting documentation. For more information, including acceptable evidence and application instructions, please visit my.UQ Deferring an Exam .
Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time. Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.

assessment_details.title: Client Communication Project: Seasonal Animal Health Information for Rural Practice
assessment_details.task_description: Veterinary technicians play a critical role in client education and preventative animal health communication in rural veterinary practice. This assessment requires students to work in small groups to design a practice-ready client information piece addressing a specific, seasonal animal health or management issue relevant to rural or production animal practice. The final product must be suitable for real-world distribution (e.g. email newsletter, Facebook post, flyer, SMS link, or practice website content) and include justification for timing, audience, and communication style. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Instructions for submission are on the VETS3018 Course Blackboard site.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Students with acceptable reasons (medical or compassionate reasons or as a result of exceptional circumstances) may apply for an extension of the deadline. Information on applying for an extension can be found here: my.UQ Applying for an extension .
Extension applications must be received by the assessment due date and time.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time.
If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example: If you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).
10% will be deducted per day, for up to 7 calendar days, at which point your submission will receive a mark of zero (0) unless an extension has been approved.

assessment_details.title: Parasitology Practical and Theory Exam
assessment_details.task_description: This exam is an on-campus invigilated practical and theory examination with a duration of 2hrs with 1hr for each section. This time does not include moving between practical and theory components of the exam that will occur in different rooms/labs and under differing examination conditions. Details of the exam components and conditions, etc, for both theory and practical will be provided during the semester. This exam accounts for a total of 20% towards the final mark for VETS3018. This exam is based on all parasitology lectures (large animal parasitology 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6) and the parasitology practical and examines different aspects of large animal parasitological knowledge. The student is expected to be able to identify parasites of importance in large animal veterinary practice and be able to describe their control and management. The results will be posted on the course Blackboard Ultra site. NOTE: The content from the parasitology lectures and practical will not be re-examined specifically in the final exam but a general knowledge of parasitology is expected as some health and management conditions in large animals will have parasites as possible differential diagnoses or a possible instigating cause. Consequently, some parasitological knowledge would be assumed for the final examination. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: End of Semester - Theory Examination
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The end of semester Final Exam will be an in-person invigilated digital Inspera exam and students will complete this exam using their own laptop. The Final Exam will be held following the lecture series, and consists of multiple-choice questions (MCQ), short answer and essay-type questions on the content presented throughout the course in lectures and practicals, excluding content from large animal parasitology specifically. NOTE: The content from the parasitology lectures and practical will not be re-examined specifically in the final exam but a good general knowledge of parasitology is expected as some heath and management conditions in large animals will have parasites as possible differential diagnoses or a possible instigating cause. Consequently, some parasitological knowledge would be assumed for the final examination. Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) This assessment task is to be completed in-person. assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assessment using the Inspera platform
Students must click the ‘SUBMIT NOW’ button on the summary page to submit their answers. After submission, a confirmation will appear on the dashboard indicating that the answers have been submitted and can no longer be edited.
For all exams lasting 60, 90, 120, or 180 minutes, an additional 10 minutes of planning time is provided to read the questions and plan responses, though students may start working immediately if they choose.
It’s crucial to ensure your laptop meets the device requirements for Inspera assessments before the exam. If you don’t have a suitable laptop, you can borrow one from the Library. Check Get Familiar with Inspera for the most up-to-date information about using the platform. Note that power outlets will not be available during exams.

Please note that there are no provisions to defer an already-deferred exam. You must be available to sit your deferred examination at the allocated time. Students who submit five or more deferral requests within a twelve-month period will be contacted with advice on the services and support available within the University.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .
learning_resources.other_course_materials: If we've listed something under further requirement, you'll need to provide your own. Required Item Description Further Requirement Coveralls, and work boots Coverall/overalls and washable protective work boots are required for entry into the large animal practicals contained within the course. Gum boots may be worn but it is preferred if work boots without shoe laces (e.g. Blundstone-type boots) are used in the practicals that involve animal handling. Entry to practicals without appropriate PPE will not occur. own item needed
learning_resources.required: Item Description Further Requirement Coveralls, and work boots Coverall/overalls and washable protective work boots are required for entry into the large animal practicals contained within the course. Gum boots may be worn but it is preferred if work boots without shoe laces (e.g. Blundstone-type boots) are used in the practicals that involve animal handling. Entry to practicals without appropriate PPE will not occur. own item needed

learning_activities.topic: Procedural skills tutorials These hands-on tutorials (3) aim to increase the skills of the students and add to lecture content. Blood draw - tutorial designed to give students comfort in the use of needles and syringes and the positioning of both body and hands on large animal models before the skills are used on live animals Equine radiology - tutorial using models to examine positioning and angles in the taking of radiographs in a field setting. Equine dentistry - tutorial using models to examine positioning, common pathologies and remedial treatment of equine dental problems. NOTE - Entry to the clinical skills HUB requires closed shoes and either laboratory coats, scrubs or clean clothing depending on the tutorial. Students will be advised on appropriate clothing during the course.

Lectures Lectures will be delivered that cover a range of topics in large animal medicine, surgery and management. These can be grouped generally into modules that will include clinical examination and procedural skills in horses cattle and small ruminants large animal clinical parasitology reproductive management, obstetrics and neonatal care in horses, cattle and small ruminants metabolic and other common disease problems of small and large ruminants equine dentistry and radiology common disease problems in horses

Live animal practicals Practicals in this course aim to increase the clinical and procedural skills in students across a range of species. There is a focus on safety within each practical to ensure confidence across species. The practicals are listed below: Cattle handling and clinical examination * Cattle procedural skills * Equine handling Equine clinical examination and procedural skills * Equine procedural skills and bandaging * Sheep handling and clinical skills * NOTE: Entry to practicals without appropriate PPE (i,e, coveralls, appropriate protective boots) will not occur. Sun protection (i.e. hat and SPF50 sunscreen) and water is highly recommended as these practicals will occur during the heat of the Queensland summer. NOTE: A stethoscope should be brought to the FIVE marked practicals (*)
Parasitology practical Laboratory based practical focusing on the identification of common large animal internal and external parasites together with management of the identified parasite.
